title: Threshold-Controlled Geometric Reorganization in 2D Bootstrap Percolation
authors: Fangfang Wang, Wei Liu, Kai Qi, Ying Tang, Zengru Di
abstract: Two-dimensional bootstrap percolation is usually characterized by bulk observables, but whether increasing the activation threshold qualitatively reorganizes the geometry of the absorbing state has remained unclear. Here we show that the response undergoes a threshold-controlled geometric crossover. At low thresholds, the extrema of bulk and boundary-sensitive observables remain confined to a single collective low-$p$ window. At high thresholds, they split into distinct branches, revealing multiple geometric response scales. Over the accessible system sizes, the dominant finite-size signatures shift from fluctuations of the final active density to non-singleton boundary observables, while the fluctuation peak itself decreases. Time-resolved mechanism traces show that this crossover is accompanied by a progression from extended collective propagation to frontier exhaustion and, at the highest threshold, to quasi-one-step stabilization. Our results identify boundary organization as the dominant structural signature of high-threshold bootstrap percolation and show that conventional bulk observables alone do not capture the full reorganization of the absorbing state.
summary: This paper discovers that increasing the activation threshold in 2D bootstrap percolation triggers a geometric crossover. While low-threshold dynamics are characterized by a single collective response, high-threshold dynamics split into multiple response scales dominated by boundary structures. This demonstrates that bulk density measurements alone are insufficient to describe the structural reorganization of the absorbing state in high-threshold systems.
main_problem: The study investigates whether increasing the activation threshold (k) in 2D bootstrap percolation qualitatively reorganizes the geometry of the absorbing state and whether bulk observables are sufficient to capture these structural changes.
main_result: The authors identify a threshold-controlled geometric crossover where high-threshold regimes (k=3, 4) exhibit multiple geometric response scales and boundary-dominated signatures, unlike the single response window seen at low thresholds (k=2).
method: Numerical simulations using synchronous bootstrap updates on square lattices of various sizes (L=30 to 180) with large-scale sampling (10^5 configurations) and finite-size scaling analysis.
model_or_system: Two-dimensional bootstrap percolation on an L x L square lattice with periodic boundary conditions, involving an irreversible, deterministic activation process based on a threshold k.
key_observables: Bulk observables (final active density, density fluctuations, mean gain) and geometric/boundary observables (boundary-edge density, non-singleton cluster site/edge densities, and isolated-site signals).
important_parameters: Activation threshold (k=1, 2, 3, 4), initial occupation probability (p), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as an irreversible, deterministic process; the study focuses on accessible system sizes and does not claim a thermodynamic higher-order transition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the bootstrap rule and the distinction between bulk and geometric observables; Figure 2 displays normalized response curves showing peak splitting; Figure 3 shows peak amplitude scaling; Figure 4 shows the shift in characteristic response positions; Figure 5 illustrates time-resolved mechanism traces.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the bootstrap model and the problem of geometric reorganization, presents numerical results for different thresholds (k=1 to 4), analyzes the scaling of bulk vs. boundary observables, provides a dynamical interpretation via mechanism traces, and concludes with a summary of the identified regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Overview of threshold-controlled geometric reorganization in two-dimensional bootstrap percolation. (a) Schematic of the bootstrap rule. Active sites remain active, while inactive sites become active if they have ≥k active neighbors, leading to irreversible evolution toward absorbing states. (b) Bulk and boundary-sensitive observables. Bulk: final active density ρ∞, density fluctuation k2(ρ), and mean gain ∆(ρ). Boundary: edge density B, non-singleton boundary sites Gns site, non-singleton boundary edges Gns edge, and isolated-site signal n. (c) Threshold-dependent response. Characteristic points show a single low-p window for k = 2, branch splitting for k = 3, and multiple...

FIG. 2. Normalized response curves of representative observ- ables in two-dimensional bootstrap percolation for thresholds k = 1–4. For k = 1 [panel (a)], the peak of k2(ρ) matches the exact result, benchmarking the numerics. For k = 2 [panel (b)], the responses of k2(ρ), Gns edge, B, and ∆ρ remain confined to a narrow low-p interval, consistent with a single primary re- sponse window. For k = 3 and k = 4 [panels (c) and (d)], the peak positions separate clearly, indicating staged geometric reorganization of the absorbing state. Different observables reach their extrema at distinct p, showing that high-threshold dynamics are governed by multiple response scales rather than a single...

FIG. 3. Logarithmic plots of the peak amplitudes of observ- ables in two-dimensional bootstrap percolation for thresholds k = 1–4. For k = 1 [panel (a)], the peak height of k2(ρ) remains close to the exact binary-response value. For k = 2 [panel (b)], the peak amplitudes show only weak size depen- dence. For k = 3 and k = 4 [panels (c) and (d)], the peak amplitudes of Gns site and Gns edge increase strongly over the avail- able size range, whereas that of k2(ρ) decreases. This contrast indicates that the dominant finite-size signatures shift toward boundary-sensitive observables at high thresholds.

FIG. 4. System-size dependence of the characteristic posi- tions p∗for different observables in two-dimensional bootstrap percolation at thresholds k = 1–4. For k = 1 [panel (a)], the numerical peak position of k2(ρ) matches the exact result. For k = 2 [panel (b)], the characteristic positions of n, B, k2(ρ), and ∆ρ remain close and shift together toward smaller p with increasing L, consistent with a single low-p response window. For k = 3 and k = 4 [panels (c) and (d)], the characteristic positions split into distinct branches, indicating multiple ge- ometric response scales at high thresholds.
